reason: Useful framing tip that distinguishes the hands-on tutorial from the authoritative OSP reference.
Genuine operational knowledge about jac run graph persistence and jac clean --all.
Bare horizontal-rule separator with no content.
Sets up the OOP-vs-OSP contrast that motivates the paradigm.
Core one-sentence definition of OSP: sending computation (walkers) to data in a graph.
Mermaid diagram illustrating the OOP-vs-OSP mental model; supports surrounding prose.

Section header introducing nodes as graph-connectable classes.
Canonical node declaration example showing has fields, methods, and object-like usage.
Key conceptual point that nodes are full classes with dormant graph capability.

Section header introducing the ++> connect operator.
Core example of connecting nodes with ++> including root as the default starting node.
Short connective prose leading into the graph diagram; harmless glue for the example.
Diagram showing the resulting graph structure of the ++> example.
Bare horizontal-rule separator with no content.
Section header introducing typed edges with data.
Core typed-edge example with the +>: Edge(...) :+> connection operator syntax.
Explains edge-carried data semantics and points to the full edges reference.

Section header introducing spatial query operators.
Core graph-query example covering [root --> ] edge references and [?:Person] type filtering.
Dense summary of edge-reference query grammar: edge-type, node-type, attribute filters, and chained hops.
Bare horizontal-rule separator with no content.
Core Root-type vs root-instance distinction essential for walker ability declarations and spawn expressions.
One-line definition of walkers as graph-traversing computation.
Canonical walker example demonstrating spawn, visit [--> ], here, and per-node-type abilities.

Expected-output block showing the walker only greets Alice, grounding the traversal lesson.
Critical semantic warning that spawned walkers do not auto-traverse without an explicit visit ability.
Explains the walker traversal semantics behind the surprising output, a core visit-model insight.

Core example of recursive traversal with visit [--> ] inside a node-entry ability.

Compact example demonstrating walker state (self) versus current node (here) during traversal.

Core report example showing result collection via result.reports from a spawned walker.
